content.problemStatement:
Write a program to print all prime numbers in the range [LLL, RRR].


Input Format

The first and only line of input contains two space separated integers, LLL and RRR - The start and end of the range.


Output Format

Print on a single line, all prime numbers between LLL and RRR (inclusive) in increasing order with a space between them.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers L and R (1 ≤ L ≤ R ≤ 1000).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print all prime numbers in the inclusive range [L, R] in increasing order, separated by a single space. If there is no prime in the range, print an empty line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There is a single test case. The primes between 2 and 11 inclusive are 2, 3, 5, 7 and 11.
Only one number (11) is in the range and it is prime, so it is printed.
The range contains only the number 1, which is not prime, therefore an empty line is printed.

solution.approach: We pre‑compute primality of every integer up to the maximum possible R (1000) using the Sieve of Eratosthenes. The sieve runs once in O(MAX_N log log MAX_N) time and stores a boolean array isPrime. For each test case we simply iterate from L to R and output the numbers whose flag is true. This yields linear time in the size of the queried interval and constant extra memory per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(MAX_N log log MAX_N + Σ(R‑L+1)) ≈ O(1000 + total numbers scanned)
solution.spaceComplexity: O(MAX_N) for the sieve array (≈1001 booleans)

code.Python3:
import sys
MAX_N=1000
is_prime=[True]*(MAX_N+1)
is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False
for p in range(2,int(MAX_N**0.5)+1):
    if is_prime[p]:
        for m in range(p*p,MAX_N+1,p):
            is_prime[m]=False

data=sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
it=iter(data)
T=int(next(it))
out_lines=[]
for _ in range(T):
    L=int(next(it)); R=int(next(it))
    primes=[str(i) for i in range(L,R+1) if is_prime[i]]
    out_lines.append(' '.join(primes))
print('\n'.join(out_lines))
